title: ISSS608-VAA
text: Welcome to ISSS608 Visual Analytics and Applications. In this website, you will find my coursework prepared for this course.

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
Use p_load() function to install the collection of R packages under tidyverse, which will include the ggplot2, often used to create data-driven graphics based on The Grammar of Graphics.

pacman::p_load(tidyverse)


exam_data <- read_csv("data/Exam_data.csv")

We got 322 rows of data with 7 attributes.

The categorical attributes are: ID, CLASS, GENDER and RACE.
The continuous attributes are: MATHS, ENGLISH and SCIENCE.

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
Use p_load() function to install the collection of R packages under tidyverse, which will include the ggplot2, often used to create data-driven graphics based on The Grammar of Graphics.

pacman::p_load(tidyverse)


exam_data <- read_csv("data/Exam_data.csv")

We got 322 rows of data with 7 attributes.

The categorical attributes are: ID, CLASS, GENDER and RACE.
The continuous attributes are: MATHS, ENGLISH and SCIENCE.

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
Histogram of Maths Scores
Here, you can compare the difference between R Graphics, the core graphical function of Base R, and ggplot plotting a histogram.

R Graphicsggplot2



hist(exam_data$MATHS)











ggplot(exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 10, color = "black", fill = "lightgrey") +
  labs(title = "Histogram of exam_data$MATHS",
       x = "exam_data$MATHS",
       y = "Frequency") +
  theme_bw()











As we can see, the difference is obvious. As quoted from Hadley Wickham:

The transferable skills from ggplot2 are not the idiosyncrasies of plotting syntax, but a powerful way of thinking about visualisation, as a way of mapping between variables and the visual properties of geometric objects that you can perceive.

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
Grammar of Graphics
The ggplot2 follows a layered grammer of graphics, the 7 layers can be described as follow:

Data: The dataset being plotted.
Aesthetics: It take attributes of the data and use them to influence visual characteristics, such as position, colours, size, shape, or transparency.
Geometrics: The visual elements used for our data, such as point, bar or line.
Facets: Split the data into subsets to create multiple variations of the same graph (paneling, multiple plots).
Statistics: Statiscal transformations that summarise data (e.g. mean, confidence intervals).
Coordinate: It defines the plane on which data are mapped on the graphic.
Themes: Modify all non-data components of a plot, such as main title, sub-title, y-aixs title, or legend background.

title: Hands-on Exercise 1
text:
Reference

Prof. Kam Tin Seong R for Visual Analytics.

title: Hands-on Exercise 2
text:
Beside tidyverse, four R packages will be used. Here is a table for the packages, their purpose and primary functions:








Package
Purpose
Primary Functions




ggrepel
Repel overlapping text and label geoms
geom_text_repel(), geom_label_repel()


patchwork
Compose multiple ggplots into a single figure
+, /, \|, plot_layout(), plot_annotation()


ggthemes
Extra themes inspired by news and science outlets
theme_economist(), theme_wsj(), theme_tufte()


hrbrthemes
Typography-centric, publication-ready themes
theme_ipsum(), theme_ipsum_rc(), theme_modern_rc()


tidyverse
Core data wrangling and ggplot2 visualisation
ggplot(), dplyr, tidyr, readr



Check the installation:

pacman::p_load(ggrepel, patchwork, 
               ggthemes, hrbrthemes,
               tidyverse) 

Import the data:

exam_data <- read_csv("data/Exam_data.csv")

title: Hands-on Exercise 2
text:
Beyond ggplot2: ggrepel
One of the challenge in plotting statistical graph is annotation, especially with large number of data points.

The plotThe code













ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS,
           y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = lm,
              size = 0.5) +
  geom_label(aes(label = ID),
             hjust = .5,
             vjust = -.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100),
                  ylim = c(0, 100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")

title: Hands-on Exercise 2
text:
Working with ggrepel

The plotThe code













ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS,
           y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = lm,
              size = 0.5) +
  geom_label_repel(aes(label = ID),
                   fontface = "bold") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100),
                  ylim = c(0, 100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")

text:

title: Hands-on Exercise 2
text:
Beyond ggplots Themes
ggplot2 comes with eight built-in themes, they are: theme_gray(), theme_bw(), theme_classic(), theme_dark(), theme_light(), theme_linedraw(), theme_minimal(), and theme_void().

The plotThe code













ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20,
                 boundary = 100,
                 color = "grey25",
                 fill = "grey90") +
  theme_gray() +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores")



Refer to this link to learn more about ggplot2 Themes

title: Hands-on Exercise 2
text:
Working with ggtheme
ggthemes provides ‘ggplot2’ themes that replicate the look of plots by Edward Tufte, Stephen Few, Fivethirtyeight, The Economist, ‘Stata’, ‘Excel’, and The Wall Street Journal, among others.
In the example below, The Economist theme is used.

The plotThe code













ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20,
                 boundary = 100,
                 color = "grey25",
                 fill = "grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_economist()

title: Hands-on Exercise 2
text:
Working with hrbrthemes

The plotThe code













ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20,
                 boundary = 100,
                 color = "grey25",
                 fill = "grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_ipsum(axis_title_size = 18,
              base_size = 15,
              grid = "Y")









TipWhat can we learn from the code chunk above?




axis_title_size argument is used to increase the font size of the axis title to 18,
base_size argument is used to increase the default axis label to 15, and
grid argument is used to remove the x-axis grid lines.

title: Hands-on Exercise 2
text:
Beyond ggplots Themes: ggthemes & hrbrthemes
ggplot2 comes with eight built-in themes, they are: theme_gray(), theme_bw(), theme_classic(), theme_dark(), theme_light(), theme_linedraw(), theme_minimal(), and theme_void().

The plotThe code













ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20,
                 boundary = 100,
                 color = "grey25",
                 fill = "grey90") +
  theme_gray() +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores")



Refer to this link to learn more about ggplot2 Themes

title: Hands-on Exercise 2
text:
Beyond Single Graph
Plotting below 3 single graphs for composition.

The plotThe code













p1 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") + 
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores")




The plotThe code













p2 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of English scores")




The plotThe code













p3 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x= MATHS, 
                 y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              size=0.5) +  
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")
